This requires building the toolset with `await strategy.createNoProxyServerToolset(NextClientRMachine)` (in place of `createServerToolset`), which emits `routeHandlers` instead of `rMachineProxy`. It is available **only on `NextAppPathStrategy`**; `routeHandlers.entrance` is the ready-made `GET` handler for the entry route. When using this mode, do **not** create `proxy.ts`.

### 11.8. `createNextDevImport` (Next.js only)

Helper for `setup.ts` that returns a development-mode module loader backed by [`jiti`](https://github.com/unjs/jiti), so that edits on resource modules (gears, shells) propagate to subsequent SSR renders **without restarting the dev server**. In production it returns `null` and the consumer falls through to a native dynamic `import(...)`.

```ts
// in pub/loader.ts (or prv/loader.ts) — route the loader through it
import { createNextDevImport } from "@r-machine/next/dev";
const devImport = await createNextDevImport(import.meta.url);
// ResourceAtlas.loader.register(["base/", ...], (path) =>
//   devImport ? devImport(`./${path}`) : import(`./${path}`));
```

**Signature:** `createNextDevImport(importMetaUrl: string): Promise<((path: string) => Promise<unknown>) | null>`.

Activation gates — returns `null` (no jiti) unless **all** hold: `NODE_ENV !== "production"`, running on the server (no `window`), not the Edge runtime, and `jiti` installed in the consumer's project.

**Why it matters.** Next/Turbopack reload their module cache on file change, but R-Machine's blueprint cache still wraps the prior factory closure, so an edit renders stale output across SSR requests until restart. Routing the loader through jiti bypasses both caches; the next request re-reads from disk. The same path makes [`verifyResourceAtlas`](#149-verifyresourceatlas) work for Next setups under vitest.

**jiti as an opt-in dev dep.** `jiti` is an *optional peer dependency* of `@r-machine/next` — install it (`pnpm add -D jiti`) only for HMR on resource modules and/or `verifyResourceAtlas` support. When missing, `createNextDevImport` returns `null`, the production import path is used, a one-shot console warning is emitted, and `verifyResourceAtlas` appends a `dev-loader-not-active` issue.

### 11.9. SSR hydration of `OuterGear` state (Next.js)

An `OuterGear` is a plain async factory (§4.9), so giving it a server-derived initial state is just ordinary code inside `define(...)` — what goes in the factory is entirely up to you. This section shows **one convenient pattern**, not a requirement: seed the state from a **server snapshot read through a port**, awaited via the canonical `_.action()` (§6.1).

The end-to-end shape (snapshot source, gear, client component):

```ts
// lib/actions.ts — the snapshot source (MUST be deterministic for the request:
// server render and client hydration both invoke it, results must be equal)
"use server";
export async function loadCartSnapshot(): Promise<{ items: string[] }> {
  return { items: ["Keyboard", "Mouse", "Monitor"] }; // prod: read DB / session
}

// r-machine/pub/outer/cart.ts — seed initial state from the snapshot
export const r = OuterGear
  .withPorts({ loadCartSnapshot })
  .withState({ items: [] as string[] })
  .define(async ({ $ }, _) => {
    _.action()(await $.ports.loadCartSnapshot());   // §6.1 canonical-action seeding
    return {
      items: _.getter(() => $.state.items),
      add:   _.action((item: string) => ({ items: [...$.state.items, item] })),
    };
  });

// components/Cart.tsx — consumed via ClientPlug; useR() suspends until seeded. Mount from a page.
const plug = ClientPlug("outer/cart");
export function Cart() {
  const [cart] = plug.useR();
  return <ul>{cart.items.map((i) => <li key={i}>{i}</li>)}</ul>;
}
Cart.plug = plug;
```

**How it avoids a hydration mismatch.** The factory runs on **both** sides — server (request scope, snapshot read in-process) and client during hydration (same read re-run). Being `async`, the client plug suspends while the snapshot resolves; the server streams the resolved HTML and hydration reuses it, so there is no mismatch **provided the snapshot is deterministic for the request**. No extra Suspense boundary needed: `@r-machine/next` builds on `@r-machine/react`, which already provides one.

**The port is your choice.** A server action is convenient because it is **isomorphic** (in-process on the server, hidden RPC on the client), so one factory body works on both sides. But anything the factory can call works (fetch wrapper, SDK client, kit value) — the factory is plain TypeScript (§4.9).

Caveats:
- **Determinism is required.** If the source varies between the two reads (wall-clock, randomness, a mutated session), renders differ and React warns. Key the data to the request.
- **The snapshot is read twice** (server + client hydration). Fine for idempotent reads; dedupe / cache if it matters.
- **`ServerPlug` cannot consume an `OuterGear`** (§10.2) — the consumer is always a `Plug` / `ClientPlug`.

---

## 12. Vertex pattern: `<VertexFrame>`

Each call to `Plug("vertex/...").useR()` in a component creates a new instance. To share that instance with descendants, the parent wraps the subtree in `<VertexFrame gear={instance}>`:

```tsx
const plug = Plug("vertex/shopping-cart");

export function CartPanel() {
  const [cart] = plug.useR();                          // creates the instance
  return <VertexFrame gear={cart}><CartHeader /><CartTotals /></VertexFrame>;
}
CartPanel.plug = plug;

// Descendant — same call shape receives the parent's instance via the frame:
export function CartTotals() {
  const [cart] = Plug("vertex/shopping-cart").useR();  // outside any frame → its own instance
  return <p>{cart.count} items</p>;
}
```

### 12.1. Duplicate vertex deps in a single Plug

A `Plug` may declare the same vertex namespace more than once — each occurrence is a separate creation request, yielding distinct instances (`Plug("vertex/cart", "vertex/cart")` → `[cartA, cartB]`; map mode discriminates by key). If a `<VertexFrame>` ancestor covers the namespace, **all** occurrences resolve to the frame's single instance (sharing wins over duplication); without a frame, each owns its own slot, state, and dispose lifecycle.

---

## 13. Component plug-attach convention

Every component or page that consumes resources hoists its plug to module scope as a plain `const plug = Plug("shell/common")` (used via `const [common] = plug.useR()` in the body), then **attaches** it to the consumer as a static property (`Comp.plug = plug`) — the plug is *not* exported. The consumer itself (the component/page/render fn carrying `.plug`) is the test-time mock target: you pass it to `mockPlug`.

### 13.1. Why attach instead of `export const plug`

1. **An unambiguous mock target.** If every consumer did `export const plug`, a test importing several of them would hit a wall of identically-named `plug` symbols — name collisions, and easy mis-imports that the types won't catch (every plug has a plausibly-similar shape). Attaching ties the plug to its consumer's *unique* name, so the mock target reads `mockPlug(CartView)` / `mockPlug(ProductPage)` — self-documenting, exactly one obvious symbol per file.

2. **HMR / Fast Refresh.** React Fast Refresh only preserves component state across edits when *every* export of a module is a component. A non-component `export const plug` makes the module ineligible and forces a full reload (losing state). Attaching leaves the module exporting only its component, so Fast Refresh keeps working.

3. **Next.js page/layout export validation.** Under the Webpack bundler, Next type-checks the *exports* of `page.tsx` / `layout.tsx` against a fixed allow-list (`default`, `metadata`, `generateMetadata`, `generateStaticParams`, route-segment config such as `dynamic` / `revalidate`, …). An extra `export const plug` is not on that list and fails the build with an invalid-export type error. Attaching the plug to the page/layout function (and leaving it unexported) sidesteps the check entirely. This makes the convention not merely preferable but **required** for pages and layouts.

---

## 14. Testing: `mockPlug`

Single primitive, uniform across families (gears, shells, a component carrying its attached plug, even a vertex). You always mock **the thing under test** — for a component, pass the component itself (its plug is attached as `Comp.plug`), not a dependency's. For a bare resource pass the resource `r`. `mockPlug(carrier)` returns `{ with, default }`; both enter the plug's machine into **test mode** (a consumer renders without a `<…RMachine>` provider) and return a **controller** that is `Disposable` (`[Symbol.dispose]` = `reset`), so `using ctrl = mockPlug(carrier).with({ … })` auto-resets at block exit (§14.4).

### 14.1. Two channels — the mental model

`mockPlug` influences the plug two ways; keeping them separate is the key to the API:

| Channel | How | What it touches | Nature |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **`.with({ … })`** | argument object, checked against the resolved shape | **resolution inputs**: the locale key (`$.locale` / `$.ambientLocale`, §14.7), `$.ports`, `$.kit`, dep-surface members | **static** — substituted once, at resolution |
| **the controller** | `ctrl.state`, `ctrl.deps.X.state`, `ctrl.kit.X.state` | the **live state** of stateful outer/vertex gears | **reactive** — drives the *real* shared cell; real getters/actions stay coherent and re-render |

State is deliberately **not** a `.with(...)` override — it's a live cell, so it lives on the controller (§14.3); `.with(...)` covers only resolution-time inputs and is structurally type-checked against the resolved shape.

### 14.2. Channel A — resolution overrides (`.with`)

Overridable: the plug's direct dependencies (by name / position), kit entries (`$.kit`), and — conditional on the resource — the locale key (named by plug kind, §14.7; gears have no locale) and `$.ports` (only resources declared with `withPorts({...})`). Anything not overridden runs through the production factory as a deep partial on top of the resulting Surface, so unsubstituted paths (including `[Symbol.dispose]` teardown) still run; `mockPlug` also reaches `$`-prefixed members (relays, internal actions).

Resource-level test — override a port (pass the resource `r`), drive the rest of the real factory:

```ts
const calls: [string, string][] = [];
using _ctrl = mockPlug(r).with({                  // `using` resets at block exit
  $: { ports: { createPost: async (t, b) => { calls.push([t, b]); } } },
});
const instance = await r.create();
await instance.submit("hello", "world");
expect(calls).toEqual([["hello", "world"]]);
```

### 14.3. Channel B — the state controller (live, reactive)

The controller carries a `state` handle for every stateful gear in reach, typed from the atlas:

- `ctrl.state` — the mocked plug's **own** state, present only when the plug is itself a stateful outer gear (declared with `withState(...)`).
- `ctrl.deps.<key>.state` — a **dependency's** state. `<key>` is the dep name (map form) or numeric index (list form), e.g. `ctrl.deps[0].state`.
- `ctrl.kit.<key>.state` — a **kit entry's** state (client kits may hold stateful outer gears).

Only stateful outer/vertex gears appear; everything else is absent from the type, so an out-of-band access is invisible in autocomplete.

**Writing is a deep-partial merge** (same shape a reducer returns): `ctrl.state = { count: 10 }` merges over current state — other keys survive, arrays replaced wholesale — it does **not** replace the whole object.

**Reading returns the full current state.** Since a write is a partial patch, the complete value exists only once resolved, so **reading `.state` before the plug resolves throws** `ERR_STATE_NOT_RESOLVED` (a loud "render/`create` first" rather than a misleading partial).

**Seed before, drive after.** A write *before* resolution is queued and applied once the cell comes to life; writes *after* publish straight to the live cell. Order in a test: seed → render/`create` → drive and read.

```ts
using ctrl = mockPlug(counter).default();

ctrl.state = { count: 10, label: "x" }; // seed: queued until resolve

const inst = await counter.create();    // cell comes to life; seed applied
expect(inst.count()).toBe(10);          // the REAL getter reads the seeded cell
expect(ctrl.state).toEqual({ count: 10, label: "x" });

ctrl.state = { count: 20 };             // live deep-partial merge — `label` survives
expect(inst.count()).toBe(20);

inst.bump(5);                           // a REAL action publishes to the same cell
expect(ctrl.state).toEqual({ count: 25, label: "x" }); // the controller observes it
```

Driving a **dependency's** state is identical through `ctrl.deps[<key>].state` (seed before render, read after — see the end-to-end example in §14.8). Because the cell is the **real, shared** one, the dependency's real getters/actions and the consumer's reactivity stay coherent — no fake surface to sync; for a vertex gear it binds to the per-consumer instance.

> In React, a write that should re-render (`ctrl.state = …`, or a real action) must run inside `act(...)` (standard RTL). The binding assumes the dependency resolves once and its cell stays stable; a re-mount or second `r.create()` creates a new cell and the handle tracks the most recent.

### 14.4. `.default()` and the lifecycle

`.default()` is exactly `.with({})`: resolve against the real defaults — no resolution override — while still returning the controller. Use it when you want real production output (a server component at its default locale, a client component without a provider) and only need to seed/observe state, or when you need nothing but test mode:

```ts
using ctrl = mockPlug(r).default(); // just enter test mode; auto-reset at scope exit
```

Every `mockPlug` call bumps the machine's test-mode refcount; the controller's **`reset()`** undoes that one mock (removes its override, clears its state binding) and, on the last exit for that machine, wipes the resolved resource state so the next test starts clean.

Three composable ways to make sure `reset()` runs:

```ts
// 1 — using (recommended): controller is Disposable (= reset), runs even if an assertion throws
using ctrl = mockPlug(r).with({ /* … */ });

// 2 — explicit reset(): when the test asserts *on* reset behavior (production restored, test mode exits)
const { reset } = mockPlug(r).default(); /* … */ reset();

// 3 — global safety net: drains any mock a test forgot to reset
afterEach(resetMockPlugs);   // from @r-machine/testing
```

With any of these in place, the render-style examples below can omit a manual `reset()`.

### 14.5–14.6. Client / Server Component test

Same shape for a Client Component and a non-page Server Component — the only difference is that a Server Component is `await`ed before rendering (`render(await LocalizedHeader())`):

```tsx
import { render, screen } from "@testing-library/react";
import { mockPlug } from "@r-machine/testing";
import { Greeting } from "./Greeting";

mockPlug(Greeting).with({ $: { ambientLocale: "it" } });

render(<Greeting />);                  // Server Component: render(await Greeting())
expect(screen.getByText("Ciao")).toBeInTheDocument();
```

### 14.7. Server Page test

`mockPlug`'s locale-override key is **named by plug kind**, because the override means three different things — the name follows the meaning, and the type exposes only the key that is true for the plug you mocked:

| You mock… | Key | Meaning | Precedence |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| a **resource** `Shell` / `shell(mono)` (pass `r`) | `$.locale` | "resolve this resource **at** this locale" | **wins** (there is nothing else to resolve from) |
| an **ambient consumer** — `Plug` / `ClientPlug` (React context) or the zero-arg `ServerPlug.useR()` (request header) | `$.ambientLocale` | the locale the *absent* ambient container would have supplied | **fallback** — an explicit locale wins |
| a **`DirectPlug`**, or `ServerPlug.useR(params)` / `useR(locale)` / `useUnboundR` | *(none)* | the locale is **always explicit**, so there is no key | the explicit `useR(locale)` always wins |

So `$.ambientLocale` fills the locale only on the ambient forms; when the consumer passes an explicit locale that locale wins and there is no override key to set (mocking it is a compile error on `DirectPlug`). For Server Pages the locale is bound from URL `params` via `plug.useR(params)` — an explicit form — so there is no `ambientLocale` to set.

To vary resolution in those explicit-locale cases, pass the locale you want (you control it) and use `mockPlug` to substitute a **dependency** instead:

```tsx
// app/[locale]/stats/page.tsx
const plug = ServerPlug("inner/visit-counter");

export default async function StatsPage({ params }: PageProps<"/[locale]/stats">) {
  const [counter] = await plug.useR(params);
  return <p>Visits: {await counter.total()}</p>;
}
StatsPage.plug = plug;

// StatsPage.test.tsx
mockPlug(StatsPage).with({
  0: { total: async () => 42 },
});

render(await StatsPage({ params: Promise.resolve({ locale: "en" }) }));
expect(screen.getByText("Visits: 42")).toBeInTheDocument();
```

Override-key convention mirrors the dep declaration form:
- list-form `ServerPlug("inner/visit-counter")` → key `0`
- map-form `ServerPlug({ counter: "inner/visit-counter" })` → key `counter`

### 14.8. End-to-end: a component test driven by real state

The payoff of the two channels together. We test `CartView` by passing **the component itself** to `mockPlug` (its plug is attached as `CartView.plug`) — not the cart gear's. `.with` sets the locale; the controller seeds the `outer/cart` dependency's real state. No fake surface: the real getters compute, the real `removeItem` action publishes, and a click re-renders through the real reactivity — no manual `rerender`. (A Next.js client test also stubs `next/navigation` hooks via `vi.mock`, omitted here.)

```tsx
it("drives the cart dependency's real state and reacts to the real removeItem", async () => {
  using ctrl = mockPlug(CartView).with({ $: { ambientLocale: "it" } });

  // Seed the dependency's state BEFORE render → applied when the cart resolves.
  ctrl.deps[0].state = {
    lines: [
      { productId: "kbd-01", name: "Mechanical Keyboard", unitPrice: 129.99, qty: 1 },
      { productId: "mon-01", name: "4K Monitor", unitPrice: 1299, qty: 2 },
    ],
  };

  await act(async () => { render(<CartView />); });
  expect(screen.getByText("2.727,99 €")).toBeInTheDocument(); // EUR-formatted total
  expect(screen.getByText("3 articoli")).toBeInTheDocument(); // pluralized (it)

  // Click "Rimuovi" → the REAL removeItem action publishes to the shared cell → re-render.
  const removeButtons = screen.getAllByRole("button", { name: "Rimuovi" });
  await act(async () => { fireEvent.click(removeButtons[1]); });

  expect(screen.queryByText("4K Monitor")).not.toBeInTheDocument();
  expect(screen.getByText("1 articolo")).toBeInTheDocument();              // singular
  expect((ctrl.deps[0].state as { lines: unknown[] }).lines).toHaveLength(1);
});
```

### 14.9. `verifyResourceAtlas`

End-to-end completeness check for a resource atlas. Catches the one bug class the type system cannot see: a key declared in `ResourceAtlas` whose resource cannot resolve at runtime — typically a missing locale variant (`en.tsx` exists, `it.tsx` missing) or a misconfigured loader. Agnostic to how `load` resolves modules: it walks every atlas key, invokes the production `load`, and asserts the result is non-undefined and matches the `{ r: ... }` shape.

```ts
import { verifyResourceAtlas } from "@r-machine/testing";
const report = await verifyResourceAtlas("./src/r-machine/setup.ts");
```

**Signature:** `verifyResourceAtlas(setupFile: string | URL, options?: { strategyExportName?: string; tsconfig?: string; loaders?: (string | URL)[] }): Promise<VerifyReport>`. `setupFile` is absolute or relative to `process.cwd()` (project root under vitest); `strategyExportName` defaults to `"strategy"`, `tsconfig` to the nearest one. `loaders` lists extra loader modules to import (side-effect) before verification — needed for a split Next setup, where `setup.ts` imports only `pub/loader` and the server-only `inner/` loader lives in `prv/loader.ts` (see the split-loader note below).

**Report shape:**

```ts
type VerifyReport = {
  ok: boolean;          // === issues.length === 0
  setupFile: string;    // absolute path
  totalChecks: number;  // shell keys × locales + non-shell keys
  issues: VerifyIssue[];
};
```

Each `VerifyIssue` is plain JSON-serializable data. Common kinds:

| `kind`                    | Cause                                                                                                                                |
| ------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| `missing-resource`        | `load(...)` returned `undefined` / `null`                                                                                            |
| `loader-error`            | `load(...)` threw                                                                                                                    |
| `invalid-module-shape`    | Returned value is missing the `{ r: ... }` shape                                                                                     |
| `atlas-extraction-failed` | Could not locate the `ResourceAtlas` class via the TS API                                                                            |
| `config-access-failed`    | Setup file does not export the expected strategy                                                                                     |
| `loader-module-failed`    | An extra module listed in `loaders` threw while being imported                                                                       |
| `dev-loader-not-active`   | Next setup used [`createNextDevImport`](#118-createnextdevimport-nextjs-only) but jiti did not activate — install hint surfaced |

Shell-keyed issues additionally carry `locale` and `isCanonical: boolean` (true when the failing locale is `defaultLocale` — distinguishes a structural break from a translation gap). All key-bound issues carry a `sourceLocation: { file, line, column }` pointing to where the key is declared in `resource-atlas.ts` — clickable from most editors and CI log viewers.

**Mechanics.** A static phase uses the TS Compiler API to walk the import graph from `setupFile`, locate `ResourceAtlas`, and extract its keys + declaration positions. A runtime phase dynamic-imports `setupFile`, reads the named strategy export, then imports any extra `loaders` for their `register()` side-effect — these land on the **same** `ResourceAtlas.loader` the config holds (the dispatcher reads the registry lazily, so the import just has to precede the key loop). It then verifies each key via the production `load`, dispatched by layout kind: `shell` → one `load` per locale (full matrix); `shell(mono)` → one against `defaultLocale`; `gear:*` → one with `locale: undefined`. The same return-shape check used in production runs here.

**Test-as-CI-gate pattern (recommended).** Place a single test next to the setup file; `vitest run` is the gate (no separate CLI, same loader env as production). On failure `expect(report.issues).toEqual([])` prints the full issue list in the diff:

```ts
// tests/r-machine/setup.test.ts
it("every declared resource resolves through the configured loader", async () => {
  // split Next setup: also pass { loaders: [import.meta.resolve("../../src/r-machine/prv/loader.ts")] } (see split-loader note below)
  const report = await verifyResourceAtlas(import.meta.resolve("../../src/r-machine/setup.ts"));
  expect(report.issues).toEqual([]);
  expect(report.totalChecks).toBeGreaterThan(0);
});
```

**Peer dep & environment:** uses the TS Compiler API, declaring `typescript >=5.0` as an **optional** peer dependency (only needed when you call `verifyResourceAtlas`). Since it invokes the *real* `load`, run it in the project's bundler-aware runner if the loader uses bundler APIs (`import.meta.glob`, Webpack contexts) — `vitest` satisfies this for Vite. For **Next.js**, ``import(`./${path}`)`` does not resolve under Vite: use [`createNextDevImport`](#118-createnextdevimport-nextjs-only) (routes loads through bundler-neutral `jiti`, activated via a process-global flag); if jiti is missing the report surfaces `dev-loader-not-active`.

**Split `pub/`/`prv/` loaders (Next.js).** A split Next setup keeps the server-only `inner/` loader in `prv/loader.ts`, imported only by `server-toolset.ts` — so importing `setup.ts` registers only `pub/loader` and `inner/` keys would fail with `loader-error` (`ERR_NO_LOADER_REGISTERED`). Pass `prv/loader.ts` via `loaders` so its `register()` lands on the same atlas loader before the check runs: `verifyResourceAtlas(import.meta.resolve("../../src/r-machine/setup.ts"), { loaders: [import.meta.resolve("../../src/r-machine/prv/loader.ts")] })`. Because `prv/loader.ts` starts with `import "server-only"` (which throws outside an RSC bundle), alias it to a no-op in the vitest config: `resolve.alias["server-only"] = "@r-machine/next/dev/no-op"`. Projects with no `inner/` resources register `["*"]` in `pub/loader` and need neither `loaders` nor the alias.

---

## 15. Diagnostics

### 15.1. Type errors

Errors of the architectural class produced by R-Machine's type system are formatted as `RMachineTypeError<"...">`. Examples:

```
RMachineTypeError<"Invalid namespaces declared in atlas shape (dropped by layout filter): *** shell_wrong/common ***">
RMachineTypeError<"Atlas keys use a reserved character in an invalid position. '@' and ':' are fully reserved; '#' is allowed only as the first character (to mark a namespace as internal). Offending keys: shell@/common">
RMachineTypeError<"Layout key 'base' must end with '/' to indicate a namespace prefix (e.g. 'base/').">
RMachineTypeError<"ReactStandardStrategy does not support InnerGear. Remove these \"gear:inner\" entries from the layout definition: *** inner1/ ***">
```

Generic TypeScript errors still occur for non-architectural mistakes (malformed action returns, bad port type signatures, etc.).

### 15.2. Runtime events

R-Machine carries an internal **event bus** emitting fine-grained diagnostic events as it resolves resources, runs updates, and tears down. It is a **tracing / observability** surface for debugging and tests, not a typed public API — names and payloads are outside the semver-stable surface and may change. The bus is **lazy and zero-cost**: until something subscribes no bus is allocated and every emit site short-circuits (the production default adds no measurable overhead).

Events carry a `type` plus a small payload, in four families: `blueprint:*` (resolution / caching), `res:*` (factory build, slot commit, disposal), `wire:*` (consumer wiring / notification), `relay:*`. Three payloads referenced elsewhere:

- `res:resolveError` — carries `chain`, the resolution path to the failing namespace (the same attribution exposed via `getResolveContext`, §10.6).
- `relay:onChangeError` — `{ relayName: string; error: unknown }` (§6.3).
- `relay:loopDetected` — `{ relayName: string; runCount: number }` (§6.5).

Both helpers take the same `target` — the `rMachine` instance or any strategy built from it (both expose the bus).

**`enableRMachineDevMode(target)`** (from `r-machine`) — the live-tracing front-end: subscribes a `console.log` handler tracing every event, returns an unsubscribe function. Development only: `if (process.env.NODE_ENV !== "production") enableRMachineDevMode(rMachine)`.

**`createEventCollector(target)`** (from `@r-machine/testing`) — the test-assertion one: subscribes and buffers every event. Returns `{ events, clear(), dispose() }` — `events` is a read-only array in emit order, `clear()` empties it keeping the subscription, `dispose()` unsubscribes (both idempotent). Create it **before** the emitting action, then assert:

```ts
const collector = createEventCollector(rMachine);
// …trigger work that runs a relay (e.g. a self-feeding loop)…
expect(collector.events.some((e) => e.type === "relay:loopDetected")).toBe(true);
collector.dispose();
```

---

## 16. File conventions

A project has two config files: **`resource-atlas.ts`** (full example in §3.1/§3.2 — `defineLayout({...})` → `class ResourceAtlas extends folders<ResourceMap>() {}`) and **`setup.ts`** (full example in §3.3/§3.4 — `RMachine.create({...})` then `createToolset()` exports + the per-framework strategy via §11.1/§11.2).

Resource modules live under one of two folders, by bundle visibility: **`pub/`** ("public", client-safe — `base`/`outer`/`vertex`/`shell`/`shell/lib`) or **`prv/`** ("private", server-only — `inner`). Each folder owns a `loader.ts` (mechanism in §3.3). The `pub/`/`prv/` segment is **filesystem-only** — atlas namespaces are unchanged (`base/config`, `inner/catalog`); the loader's location absorbs it.

```
src/r-machine/
├─ resource-atlas.ts
├─ setup.ts
├─ pub/            # client-safe — loader registered from setup.ts
│  ├─ loader.ts
│  ├─ base/  outer/  vertex/
│  └─ shell/  (+ shell/lib/)
└─ prv/            # server-only — loader registered from server-toolset.ts (import "server-only")
   ├─ loader.ts
   └─ inner/
```

Per-resource files (the path shows the folder; the namespace omits the `pub/`/`prv/` segment):

```ts
// pub/<family>/<name>.ts — gear file (family ∈ base | outer | vertex);
// server-only inner gears live at prv/inner/<name>.ts
import { InnerGear | BaseGear | OuterGear, type RShape } from "@/r-machine/setup";
export const r = /* composer chain */.define(/* factory */);
export type Some_Name = RShape<typeof r>;

// pub/shell/<name>/<locale>.tsx — shell variant file
export const r = localized("shell/<name>", { /* keys */ });
```

---

## 17. Resource placement reference

| If X is… | Place it as |
|---|---|
| Locale-dependent text content with translations | Multi-locale `shell` (`pub/shell/<name>/<locale>.tsx` files) |
| Locale-aware behavior without translations (formatters, parsers) | `shell(mono)` (single file at `pub/shell/lib/<name>.ts`) |
| Reactive/stateful, consumed in the React tree at application scope | `gear:outer` (with `withState`) |
| Stateful UI whose initial state comes from a server snapshot (SSR hydration) | `gear:outer` seeded inside the factory from a port (e.g. a server action) via `_.action()(await $.ports.…)` (see §11.9) |
| Component-local reactive state consumed by descendants | `gear:outer(vertex)` consumed inside a `<VertexFrame>` |
| Per-component reactive state used only within the component itself | `gear:outer(vertex)` consumed without `<VertexFrame>` |
| Stateless service used by other gears, never by shells | `gear:base` |
| Stateless service that translations need to read | `gear:base`, listed in `bridgeGears` |
| Cross-cutting service every gear/shell needs | `gear:base` referenced from `gearKit` and/or `shellKit` |
| Server-only service (DB, secret) | `gear:inner` (server-only — lives under `prv/inner/`) |
| One-shot async resource (fetched once) | `Shell.define(async () => …)` or `OuterGear.define(async () => …)` |
| Side effect that fires on state change | `_.relay` inside the owning outer gear |
| Pure derived value over state | `_.getter(() => …)` (or `_.cell(() => …)` for memoized + fine-grained) |
| External function / server action / SDK client / fetch wrapper used inside a gear or shell | `.withPorts({ name })`, accessed via `$.ports.name` |
| Same logic with a different external binding or starting state | `r.withPorts(...).clone()` / `r.withState(...).clone()` (see §5) |
| Sibling locale variant that differs only in a handful of phrases | `r.clone((res, plugin) => ({ ...res, /* overrides */ }))` (see §5.3.4) |

---

## 18. Scalability (verified)

TS generics scale **sub-linearly**: going 10→500 resources (a 50× increase) grows type instantiations only ~5.6× (123k→686k), and per-resource cost *drops* (12.3k→1.4k inst/resource).

These numbers are **reproducible by anyone**: clone the R-Machine monorepo and run `pnpm bench:types` (declared in the root `package.json`). The harness generates realistic projects of growing size and measures tsc compile cost, type-trace hotspots, and live tsserver IntelliSense latency, so you can re-verify the claims on your own machine.

Full benchmark + methodology: https://github.com/codecarvings/r-machine/tree/main/benchmarks/type-scale — last generated report: https://github.com/codecarvings/r-machine/blob/main/benchmarks/type-scale/results/REPORT.md

---
